fields.name: Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD)
fields.definition: A disorder marked by uncontrollable, recurring thoughts (obsessions), repetitive behaviors or mental acts performed in response to those thoughts (compulsions), or both. Obsessions cause significant anxiety; compulsions are performed to reduce that anxiety, but provide only temporary relief and often reinforce the cycle. To be diagnosed, the obsessions and/or compulsions must be time-consuming (more than 1 hour per day) or cause significant distress or functional impairment. OCD is classified in the DSM-5 under Obsessive-Compulsive and Related Disorders.
fields.example: A person spends 3 hours each morning checking that appliances are unplugged, driven by an obsessive fear of causing a fire — temporarily relieved by checking, only to feel the urge return.

fields.name: Obsessions (OCD)
fields.definition: Repeated, persistent, unwanted thoughts, urges, or mental images that intrude into consciousness and cause significant distress. Obsessions in OCD are ego-dystonic — they feel foreign to the person's identity and values, unlike preferences or worries. The person typically tries to ignore, suppress, or neutralize them with a compulsion. Obsessions are not simply excessive worry about real-life problems; they are intrusive mental events that feel uncontrollable and are accompanied by intense anxiety.
fields.example: A devoted parent is tormented by intrusive thoughts about harming their child — thoughts they find deeply disturbing and contrary to their values, but cannot stop.

fields.name: Compulsions (OCD)
fields.definition: Repetitive behaviors or mental acts that a person feels driven to perform in response to an obsession or according to rigid rules. The purpose is to reduce anxiety or prevent a feared outcome — but the connection between the compulsion and the feared event is either unrealistic or excessive. Compulsions can be overt (visible behaviors like hand-washing, checking, counting, ordering) or covert (mental acts like silently repeating words, praying, or mentally reviewing events). Performing compulsions provides temporary relief but reinforces the OCD cycle.
fields.example: After touching a doorknob, washing hands exactly 7 times using a specific sequence — deviating from the sequence means starting over from the beginning.

fields.title: Common Obsession Themes in OCD
fields.body: OCD obsessions cluster around several recurring themes: Contamination — fear of germs, illness, chemicals, or being 'tainted'; Harm — fear of hurting oneself or others (accidentally or intentionally), despite having no desire to do so; Symmetry and order — an intense need for things to be 'just right,' aligned, or balanced; Forbidden or taboo thoughts — intrusive sexual, violent, or blasphemous thoughts that are deeply distressing and contrary to the person's values; Doubt and uncertainty — 'What if I didn't lock the door / turn off the stove / hit someone while driving?'; and Religious or moral scrupulosity — excessive worry about sinning, offending God, or acting immorally. The specific theme does not determine the severity of OCD — any theme can be highly disabling.

fields.title: Common Compulsion Types in OCD
fields.body: Compulsions in OCD fall into several common categories: Washing and cleaning — excessive hand-washing, showering, or cleaning objects to reduce contamination fears; Checking — repeatedly verifying that doors are locked, appliances are off, or that harm has not occurred; Counting — performing actions a specific number of times, or counting to a 'safe' number; Arranging and ordering — positioning objects in precise alignments until they feel 'right'; Repeating — repeating phrases, prayers, or actions until they feel correct; Reassurance seeking — asking others repeatedly for confirmation that nothing bad happened; and Mental rituals — mentally reviewing past events or repeating phrases internally. Most people with OCD have multiple compulsion types, and they typically change over time.

fields.name: Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP)
fields.definition: A specialized form of Cognitive Behavioral Therapy considered the gold standard psychotherapy for OCD. ERP involves two components: Exposure — deliberately and repeatedly confronting feared situations, objects, thoughts, or images that trigger obsessions; and Response Prevention — resisting the urge to perform the usual compulsion after exposure. By preventing the compulsive response, the person learns that anxiety peaks and then naturally decreases without the compulsion — and that feared outcomes do not occur. About 70% of people with OCD respond to ERP, medication, or a combination.
fields.example: A person with contamination OCD touches a 'contaminated' doorknob and then waits — without washing — until their anxiety subsides naturally, repeating the process until the trigger no longer provokes extreme fear.

fields.body: A therapist and patient first create a hierarchy of feared situations, ranked from least to most distressing. Beginning with lower-ranked items, the patient deliberately encounters the feared trigger (exposure) while refraining from performing the usual compulsion (response prevention). Anxiety typically rises, peaks, and then naturally declines — a process called habituation. With repeated practice, the trigger's ability to provoke intense anxiety weakens. The therapist coaches the patient through this process in sessions, then assigns home practice. The hierarchy is gradually worked up to more challenging items. ERP can also address purely mental compulsions by exposing patients to obsessive thoughts while preventing covert neutralizing rituals.
fields.name: How ERP Works
fields.example: Week 1: Touch a 'dirty' counter and wait 5 minutes before washing. Week 4: Touch a public restroom door handle, use no hand sanitizer, and eat lunch.

fields.name: SSRIs for OCD
fields.definition: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) are the primary medication treatment for OCD. Because OCD involves serotonin dysregulation, SSRIs can reduce the frequency and intensity of obsessions and the urge to perform compulsions. Notably, the doses used for OCD are often higher than those used to treat depression, and response may take 8–12 weeks. Commonly prescribed SSRIs for OCD include fluoxetine (Prozac), fluvoxamine (Luvox), paroxetine (Paxil), and sertraline (Zoloft). Clomipramine (a tricyclic antidepressant with strong serotonin action) is also effective for OCD.
fields.example: A person with OCD is titrated up to a higher-than-usual fluoxetine dose over 3 months and notices a 40% reduction in time spent on compulsions.

fields.question: How common is OCD?
fields.answer: Approximately 1.2% of US adults experience OCD in any given year, based on NIMH statistics. The lifetime prevalence is approximately 2.3%. OCD affects males and females nearly equally, though males are more likely to have onset in childhood and females in adolescence or early adulthood. OCD occurs across all racial, ethnic, and socioeconomic groups.

fields.question: What distinguishes OCD from being a 'neat freak' or having strong preferences?
fields.answer: OCD is distinguished by: (1) Ego-dystonia — obsessions feel intrusive and unwanted, not consistent with who the person is; (2) Driven compulsions — behaviors performed to reduce anxiety, not because the person genuinely wants to; (3) Significant time cost — typically more than 1 hour per day; (4) Functional impairment — OCD interferes with work, relationships, or daily life; and (5) Intense distress — the anxiety involved is not mild preference but severe and unrelenting. Someone who likes their desk tidy is not experiencing OCD; someone who spends 2 hours re-arranging items because 'it doesn't feel right' and cannot leave until it does may be.

fields.question: Do people with OCD know their obsessions are irrational?
fields.answer: Most people with OCD have good insight — they recognize intellectually that their obsessions are excessive or unrealistic. Despite this, they feel unable to simply stop the anxiety or the compulsions. This gap between knowing and feeling is one of the defining features of OCD. A small proportion have poor insight or overvalued ideation (strong belief the fears are realistic). DSM-5 allows for specification of insight level in diagnosis: good or fair insight, poor insight, or absent insight/delusional beliefs.

fields.name: Body Dysmorphic Disorder (BDD)
fields.definition: A condition closely related to OCD in which a person becomes obsessively preoccupied with a perceived physical flaw that is either minor or invisible to others. The person may spend hours each day checking mirrors, seeking reassurance, camouflaging the perceived flaw, or comparing their appearance to others. BDD causes significant distress and impairment. It shares OCD's obsessive-compulsive cycle and responds to similar treatments (ERP and SSRIs at higher doses). BDD is classified alongside OCD in the DSM-5 Obsessive-Compulsive and Related Disorders chapter.
fields.example: A person spends 4 hours a day examining and touching their nose in mirrors, convinced it is grotesquely large despite it being unremarkable to everyone else, and avoids social situations because of this preoccupation.

fields.title: OCD and Related Conditions (DSM-5)
fields.body: Since DSM-5, several conditions are grouped with OCD in the Obsessive-Compulsive and Related Disorders chapter because they share the core feature of obsessive preoccupation and/or repetitive behaviors: Body Dysmorphic Disorder (BDD) — obsessive focus on a perceived physical flaw; Hoarding Disorder — persistent difficulty discarding possessions, leading to unsafe accumulation (distinct from OCD-driven hoarding); Trichotillomania (Hair-Pulling Disorder) — recurrent, compulsive hair pulling causing hair loss, not driven by obsessions; Excoriation (Skin-Picking) Disorder — recurrent skin picking causing lesions; and Substance/Medication-Induced Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder. Each condition has distinct features but overlapping mechanisms, and many respond to ERP-based therapy and/or SSRIs.

fields.question: How long does it typically take for someone with OCD to receive effective treatment after onset?
fields.answer: Research estimates an average gap of 14 to 17 years between the onset of OCD symptoms and the start of effective treatment. Contributing factors include: shame and secrecy around symptoms (especially with taboo obsession themes), misdiagnosis as generalized anxiety or depression, lack of clinician familiarity with ERP, and fear that disclosing intrusive thoughts (about harm, sex, or religion) will lead to judgment. OCD symptoms typically begin in late childhood, adolescence, or early adulthood, so this delay means many people spend years suffering without appropriate care.

fields.question: Does OCD just mean being very neat, organized, or detail-oriented?
fields.answer: No. NIMH defines OCD as "a long-lasting disorder in which a person experiences uncontrollable and recurring thoughts (obsessions), engages in repetitive behaviors (compulsions), or both." The defining features are involuntary distress and impairment: people with OCD spend more than an hour per day on obsessions and compulsions and feel no pleasure from them — only temporary relief from anxiety. NIMH notes that "not all repeated thoughts are obsessions, and not all rituals or habits are compulsions" — the distinction is that OCD symptoms are uncontrollable, time-consuming, and cause genuine suffering. Preferring an organized desk is not OCD.
